title: Does CLIP’s generalization performance mainly stem from high train-test similarity?

reviews.summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.
reviews.strengths: The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.
reviews.weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.
reviews.questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.

Strengths:
The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.

Weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.

Questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: On CLIP's Out-of-Distribution Generalization

## Summary

This paper investigates a fundamental question about CLIP's remarkable out-of-distribution generalization capabilities: whether this strong performance stems from genuine generalization ability or merely reflects high similarity between training and test distributions. Through a series of well-designed pruning experiments, the authors attempt to disentangle these factors. While the work is clearly presented and methodologically sound, concerns about the limited experimental scope prevent this from being a strong contribution.

## Strengths

The paper is exceptionally well-written with clear exposition throughout. The experimental design is rigorous and well-motivated, with each experiment building logically toward the central research question. The authors demonstrate careful consideration of their methodology, employing multiple pruning strategies that are clearly defined and appropriately justified. This attention to experimental clarity is commendable and makes the work accessible and reproducible.

The core motivation is compelling: understanding whether CLIP's generalization is "real" or an artifact of train-test similarity is an important question for the community. The authors tackle this through a principled approach that is both intuitive and technically sound.

## Weaknesses

The primary limitation of this work is its narrow experimental scope. The analysis is confined almost entirely to ImageNet and its variants (ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R, etc.), which represent a relatively homogeneous class of distribution shifts—all fundamentally rooted in the same visual domain and dataset lineage. This raises serious questions about the generalizability of the findings.

The paper does not explore OOD datasets with fundamentally different characteristics, such as iWILDCam or FMoW, which exhibit different types of distribution shifts (e.g., geographic shifts, temporal shifts, domain-specific shifts). Without such experiments, it remains unclear whether the conclusions about train-test similarity explaining CLIP's performance hold beyond ImageNet-based benchmarks. The authors' central claim about CLIP's generalization mechanisms may be specific to a particular class of distribution shifts rather than broadly applicable.

Additionally, the technical choices warrant further investigation. The paper relies exclusively on CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings for computing similarity and filtering training data. Larger CLIP models may produce substantially different embeddings with different properties, potentially leading to different conclusions about which data points should be pruned. This choice is not adequately justified or explored.

## Suggestions for Improvement

To strengthen this work, the authors should:

1. **Expand dataset diversity**: Include experiments on OOD datasets unrelated to ImageNet (e.g., iWILDCam, FMoW) to determine whether the findings generalize beyond ImageNet-based distribution shifts. This would substantially increase the impact and scope of the claims.

2. **Test on heterogeneous distribution shifts**: Include datasets that exhibit different types of shifts (geographic, temporal, domain-specific) rather than variations of the same visual classification task.

3. **Investigate embedding model choice**: Ablate the analysis with larger CLIP models (e.g., ViT-L/14, ViT-g/14) to determine whether the embedding model used for similarity computation affects which data points are filtered and, consequently, the conclusions drawn.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a solid contribution to understanding CLIP's generalization properties through careful experimentation and clear presentation. However, the restriction to ImageNet-based datasets is a significant limitation that undermines the generalizability of the findings. While the work provides valuable insights for the specific case of ImageNet distribution shifts, it leaves open the critical question of whether these insights extend to other types of OOD data and distribution shifts. The paper is above the acceptance threshold due to its methodological rigor and clear presentation, but the limited scope prevents it from being a strong contribution.

The authors should consider the suggestions above as opportunities to substantially strengthen this work and broaden its impact.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.
reviews.questions: What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: CLIP's Out-of-Distribution Generalization and Train-Test Similarity

## Summary

This paper investigates an important and underexplored question: whether CLIP's strong out-of-distribution generalization can be attributed to train-test distribution similarity. The authors conduct an empirical study by removing samples similar to test sets from the training data and evaluating whether performance degradation occurs. Their surprising finding—that CLIP maintains robust OOD performance even after removing highly similar training samples—challenges conventional assumptions about the role of train-test similarity in generalization. Additionally, the discovery that a 100M subset of LAION-400M can achieve comparable performance to the full dataset has practical implications for efficient model training.

## Strengths

1. **Novel Perspective**: This is the first work to systematically examine CLIP's OOD generalization through the lens of train-test distribution similarity, addressing a question of genuine scientific interest.

2. **Compelling Empirical Findings**: The core results are intriguing and well-presented. The observation that removing nearest neighbor samples does not substantially degrade OOD performance is counter-intuitive and challenges existing hypotheses about what drives CLIP's generalization.

3. **Well-Designed Experiments**: The experimental methodology is generally sound and the results are presented clearly. The authors demonstrate careful experimental design in their approach to isolating the effect of sample similarity.

4. **Practical Value**: The finding that a 100M subset of LAION-400M maintains full-dataset performance has immediate practical applications for reducing computational costs in training vision-language models.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Limited Scope of Evaluation**: The evaluation is restricted almost entirely to ImageNet-based classification benchmarks. This narrow focus raises serious questions about the generalizability of the findings beyond this specific domain. CLIP is widely used for diverse applications beyond classification, yet these are not explored.

2. **Missing Retrieval Task Evaluation**: Retrieval tasks represent a fundamental use case for CLIP models and are essential for validating claims about the model's generalization properties. The complete absence of experiments on retrieval (e.g., using MSCOCO as in-domain and Flickr-30k as out-of-domain) is a significant limitation.

3. **Insufficient Coverage of Benchmarks**: The paper does not evaluate on diverse benchmarks such as VTAB, which would provide a more comprehensive picture of generalization behavior across different visual domains and task types.

4. **Critical Missing Technical Details**: Several crucial experimental specifications are either missing entirely or relegated to the appendix:
   - How exactly are nearest neighbor sets constructed?
   - How many samples are selected for the nearest neighbor analysis?
   - What CLIP similarity score threshold is used?
   
   These details are essential for reproducibility and should appear in the main text.

5. **Incomplete Distributional Analysis**: The paper does not compare the full similarity distributions of nearest neighbors between the filtered LAION dataset and ImageNet-Train. This is a critical omission, as it could reveal whether LAION maintains advantages through higher density of moderately-similar samples, even if maximum similarities are matched.

6. **Potential Confound Not Addressed**: A key concern is whether LAION contains a higher density of samples in the moderately-similar range compared to ImageNet, which could provide an unfair advantage and undermine the validity of the conclusions. This possibility is not adequately investigated.

## Minor Weaknesses and Suggestions

- The paper would benefit from explicit discussion of why the findings hold for CLIP but may or may not generalize to other vision-language models.
- A more thorough analysis of the similarity score threshold selection would strengthen the work.
- Discussion of the practical implications for data curation strategies could enhance the paper's impact.

## Recommendations for Improvement

To strengthen this work, the authors should:

1. Extend experiments to include retrieval tasks using standard benchmarks, as these are fundamental to CLIP's design and use cases.

2. Evaluate on additional diverse benchmarks (VTAB and others) to demonstrate that findings generalize beyond ImageNet-based classification.

3. Move complete experimental details regarding nearest neighbor construction to the main paper, including sample counts and similarity thresholds.

4. Provide a comprehensive comparison of nearest neighbor similarity distributions between filtered LAION and ImageNet-Train, examining not just the closest samples but the entire distribution.

5. Directly investigate whether LAION exhibits unfair density advantages in the moderately-similar sample range, which could confound the conclusions.

## Overall Assessment

The paper makes a valuable contribution by investigating CLIP's generalization from a fresh perspective and providing surprising empirical findings. The core insight—that train-test similarity may not be the primary driver of CLIP's OOD generalization—is important and well-motivated. However, the work suffers from a limited evaluation scope that raises questions about generalizability. The restriction to classification tasks, absence of retrieval evaluation, and missing technical details weaken the contribution. Most critically, potential confounds related to sample density distributions are not adequately addressed, which could compromise the validity of the main conclusions.

Despite these limitations, the novelty of the investigation, the quality of the core experimental design, and the practical value of the findings warrant publication. The work will likely inspire follow-up research and discussion in the community. However, addressing the evaluation scope and technical clarity issues would substantially strengthen the contribution.

## Recommendation

**Accept (marginally above threshold)** – The paper addresses an important question with novel insights and sound methodology, but would benefit significantly from expanded evaluation, retrieval task inclusion, and resolution of potential confounds. The work makes a sufficient contribution to merit publication, though addressing the noted limitations would increase its impact and robustness.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.
reviews.strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"
reviews.questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: CLIP's Out-of-Distribution Generalization

## Summary

This paper investigates whether CLIP's superior performance on out-of-distribution datasets can be attributed solely to train-test similarity. The authors propose a pruning methodology to remove training samples similar to test images and evaluate CLIP's performance on the resulting datasets. While the core finding—that CLIP maintains significant performance advantages even after reducing train-test similarity—is valuable, the paper's methodology raises fundamental concerns about whether it truly enables fair OOD evaluation.

## Strengths

1. **Meaningful empirical finding**: The observation that CLIP's OOD robustness cannot be fully explained by train-test similarity is insightful and contributes to our understanding of large vision-language models. This finding has clear implications for the community.

2. **Comprehensive experimental validation**: The paper presents multiple computationally intensive experiments with substantial effort invested in evaluation. These results provide concrete evidence supporting the main claims and will be useful for future research.

3. **Community contribution**: The release of pruned datasets and code is commendable and will facilitate follow-up work on understanding CLIP's generalization properties.

## Major Weaknesses

The paper's central limitation is methodological: the proposed approach does not achieve genuine OOD evaluation as the term is typically understood. Specifically:

1. **Domain-level vs. sample-level pruning**: The paper removes training samples that are similar to individual test images, but this does not guarantee removal of the entire test domain from the training set. True OOD evaluation requires that the complete domain distribution be held out, not merely individual similar instances. For example, if test samples consist of sketches of airplanes, removing only sketches similar to specific test airplanes leaves other dissimilar sketches of airplanes in the training set. CLIP may still learn sketch-specific features from these remaining instances.

2. **Incomplete domain removal**: The distinction between removing per-sample similarities and removing all instances of a domain is critical. Even after pruning, other samples from the same domain (e.g., other sketches, other paintings) may remain if they fall below the similarity threshold. This means the domain itself is not necessarily unseen by CLIP during training.

3. **Domain presence in training data**: There is no guarantee that the test domain (e.g., the "sketch" domain) was entirely absent from the original training data. CLIP may have encountered sketches of various objects—including those not in ImageNet—during pre-training. This undermines the claim that the domain is truly out-of-distribution.

4. **Terminology concerns**: The use of "out-of-distribution generalization" remains problematic even with the proposed methodology, as true OOD would require explicit domain-level removal from training.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Presentation clarity**: The abstract and contributions list could be more concise and clearly articulated. Some key distinctions (such as the difference between ID and OOD evaluation) are not sufficiently emphasized in the introduction.

2. **Terminology collision**: The term "similarity gap" may conflict with usage in other contexts and could benefit from clarification or renaming.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Implement domain-level pruning**: To achieve true OOD evaluation, remove all images belonging to the test domain (e.g., all sketch-style images) rather than only those similar to specific test samples. For ImageNet-Sketch, this would mean removing all sketch images from the training set.

2. **Conduct proper OOD experiments**: Perform additional experiments that explicitly remove entire domains from training and evaluate on held-out domains. This would provide more convincing evidence about CLIP's true OOD capabilities.

3. **Refine terminology**: Either adopt terminology that more accurately reflects the evaluation setting (e.g., "ID evaluation with reduced similarity") or implement the necessary changes to support claims of OOD generalization.

4. **Clarify scope**: Be explicit about whether the paper's conclusions apply to in-distribution evaluation with reduced train-test overlap versus true out-of-distribution evaluation. These are distinct evaluation regimes with different implications.

## Overall Assessment

The paper addresses an important question about CLIP's generalization and presents a valuable empirical finding: CLIP's performance advantages persist even when train-test similarity is reduced. However, the methodology does not fully support claims about OOD generalization. The per-sample similarity-based pruning approach is insufficient for domain-level distribution shift evaluation, as it may leave domain-relevant samples in the training set. 

Despite this limitation, the paper makes a contribution to understanding CLIP's behavior and provides useful resources to the community. The experimental work is substantial and the findings, while not definitively addressing OOD generalization, do offer insights into the relationship between train-test similarity and CLIP's performance. With revisions addressing the methodological concerns and clarifying the evaluation setting, this work would be more compelling.

The paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold due to its interesting findings and useful contributions, but the methodological limitations prevent it from making a fully convincing case about OOD generalization.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.
reviews.strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?
reviews.questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? reviews.text:
# Peer Review: LAION Training Data and CLIP's Out-of-Distribution Generalization

## Summary

This paper investigates whether CLIP's superior out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization compared to ImageNet-trained models can be attributed to the presence of semantically and stylistically similar images in the LAION-400M training dataset. While the work provides useful empirical evidence that LAION contains more similar samples to OOD benchmarks than ImageNet-Train, the conclusions about what fundamentally drives CLIP's generalization remain insufficiently supported. The experimental design leaves critical questions unanswered, and the practical implications of the findings are unclear.

## Strengths

The paper makes several valuable empirical contributions. Figure 1 provides a compelling visual demonstration that LAION-400M contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks, whereas the standard ImageNet training set does not. This observation is substantiated quantitatively in Figure 3 (left), which shows that nearest neighbors of OOD datasets found in LAION-400M are on average more similar than those from ImageNet-Train, with the exception of ImageNet-Val.

Additionally, Figure 3 (right) clearly demonstrates a positive correlation between CLIP's performance on samples and the availability of similar training examples, suggesting relevance of the similarity hypothesis. Figure 4 further strengthens this narrative by showing that pruning LAION for samples similar to specific OOD benchmarks (ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val) results in substantial accuracy drops compared to random pruning, indicating that these similar samples contribute meaningfully to CLIP's performance.

## Major Weaknesses

Despite these strengths, the paper falls short of establishing its central claim. The most critical issue is that **the results do not conclusively demonstrate that high train-test similarity is the primary driver of OOD generalization**. The fact that CLIP performs worse after pruning similar samples does not eliminate alternative explanations. Most tellingly, Figure 7 reveals that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val substantially hurts performance not only on those specific benchmarks but across all other ImageNet OOD variants. This cross-dataset degradation suggests these samples may be important for understanding the general ImageNet distribution rather than providing benchmark-specific shortcuts.

A crucial missing experiment undermines the entire comparative framing: **the paper does not apply the same pruning procedure to ImageNet-trained models and compare relative accuracy drop rates**. Without this comparison, we cannot determine whether CLIP's sensitivity to similarity-based pruning is unique or whether standard ImageNet-trained models exhibit comparable behavior. This would be essential for supporting the claim that CLIP's OOD generalization is distinctively explained by seeing similar training data.

The paper also lacks specificity about the scope of pruning effects. The authors should demonstrate that pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch only impacts ImageNet-Sketch performance (or at least primarily impacts it), not all benchmarks. The current results suggest a more complex relationship than the similarity hypothesis alone can explain.

## Conceptual and Methodological Issues

Several fundamental conceptual issues require clarification. The paper does not adequately define what constitutes "zero-shot" learning in the context of CLIP, nor does it clearly articulate what "out-of-distribution robustness" means given that CLIP appears to have seen similar data during training. These definitional gaps have significant implications for how the community should interpret and use OOD benchmarks.

Additionally, the practical utility of the proposed approach is unclear and potentially problematic. Why would practitioners want to intentionally prune training data to achieve worse performance on benchmarks? The motivation for Sections 5 and 6, which explore such pruning strategies, is not adequately justified. Furthermore, there are potential ethical and legal concerns: the proposed methodology may constitute transductive learning (using test data to influence training), which could violate dataset licenses such as ObjectNet's restrictions on indirect exposure to test sets.

## Minor Weaknesses and Suggestions

The paper would benefit from expanded discussion of its implications for the community. Given the findings, what should practitioners do with OOD benchmarks for evaluation? How should we reconsider the notion of "zero-shot" evaluation when models may have encountered similar training data? These questions deserve substantive treatment rather than brief mentions.

The authors should also provide a clearer motivation for their pruning methodology and address the transductive learning concerns explicitly. If the approach involves any form of test set exposure during training decisions, this must be acknowledged and discussed in relation to standard evaluation practices.

## Missing Experiments

To strengthen the conclusions, the following experiments are essential:
1. Apply the same pruning procedure to ImageNet-trained models and compare sensitivity to similarity-based pruning against CLIP models
2. Verify that pruning for specific benchmarks primarily affects performance on those benchmarks rather than all OOD tasks
3. Provide deeper analysis of why cross-dataset effects occur and what they reveal about the role of similarity versus general distribution understanding

## Recommendation

While this paper makes a useful empirical contribution by documenting the presence of similar images in LAION relative to OOD benchmarks, it does not sufficiently support its central claim about what drives CLIP's generalization. The missing comparative analysis with ImageNet-trained models, the unexplained cross-dataset effects in Figure 7, and unresolved conceptual questions about zero-shot and OOD evaluation prevent the work from reaching the acceptance threshold. The practical utility of the proposed approach also remains unclear. The paper would be substantially strengthened by addressing these gaps, particularly through comparative experiments that isolate whether CLIP's behavior is truly distinctive.

**Recommendation: Marginally below acceptance threshold**

The work has merit and addresses an important question, but requires significant revisions to the experimental design and conceptual framing before it can make a conclusive contribution to our understanding of CLIP's generalization properties.

Insufficient discussion of fundamental concepts: what constitutes 'zero-shot' and what defines 'out-of-distribution robustness'
Unclear implications for the community regarding how to use OOD benchmarks and evaluation methodology if CLIP has seen similar training data
Unclear motivation for Section 5 and 6: why would practitioners want to prune training data to achieve worse performance on benchmarks that don't directly appear in the training set

Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val hurts performance on all other ImageNet OOD datasets, suggesting these samples may be important for general ImageNet distribution understanding rather than specific benchmark similarity
suggestions: Perform similar pruning procedure on ImageNet training set and compare accuracy drop rates to CLIP models to determine if OOD generalization is uniquely explained by seeing similar samples
Clarify the notion of 'zero-shot' and 'out-of-distribution robustness' and discuss implications for community practices with OOD benchmarks
Expand discussion on what the community should do with OOD datasets and evaluation benchmarks given the findings
Provide clearer motivation for why practitioners would want to use the pruned datasets that perform worse on benchmarks
Address whether the proposed method constitutes transductive learning and potential violations of dataset licenses (e.g., ObjectNet license restrictions)
main_points: The paper demonstrates LAION contains more similar images to OOD benchmarks than ImageNet-Train, but this does not conclusively prove that high train-test similarity is the main driver of CLIP's generalization

Key experimental comparison is missing: does ImageNet-trained models show similar or different accuracy drop rates when similar pruning is applied, which would clarify if CLIP's OOD performance is uniquely due to similar training samples
The practical utility and ethical implications of the proposed pruning approach are unclear - why optimize for worse performance on test benchmarks

Results showing cross-benchmark effects (pruning for one benchmark hurts others) suggest the importance of these samples may be for general distribution understanding rather than specific benchmark similarity
Fundamental questions about what 'zero-shot' and 'OOD robustness' mean need clearer discussion given the findings
technical_details: Figure 4 results show near-pruning causes substantial drops but cross-dataset effects in Figure 7 complicate interpretation
Missing ablation: applying same pruning methodology to ImageNet to compare relative sensitivity to similarity-based pruning between CLIP and standard models
Potential transductive learning concern: test datasets may be seen directly or indirectly during training/pruning, violating assumptions of some dataset licenses like ObjectNet

The claim that high train-test similarity is insufficient to explain performance is based on models still performing well after pruning, but alternative explanations (general distribution understanding) are not ruled out

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.
reviews.strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.
reviews.weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.
reviews.questions: See weakness above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Hierarchical Diffuser for Offline Reinforcement Learning

## Summary

This paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), which extends the Diffuser framework by decomposing planning into two levels: a high-level diffuser that generates sub-goals and a low-level diffuser that refines action sequences between consecutive sub-goals. The work demonstrates empirical improvements over the baseline Diffuser across multiple benchmarks. However, the contribution is primarily incremental, combining existing diffusion-based planning techniques without substantial algorithmic innovation. While the empirical results are encouraging, concerns about the approach's fundamental design choices and missing technical clarity limit the significance of this work.

## Strengths

The paper makes several solid empirical and methodological contributions. First, the discretization of planning into sub-goals enables the approach to handle more diverse scenarios effectively. The low-level diffusion planning remains task-agnostic, which is a desirable property for generalization. 

The empirical validation is thorough and multi-faceted. The authors demonstrate that the Sparse Diffuser (SD) baseline alone outperforms standard Diffuser due to an increased receptive field, and they appropriately validate this by showing that larger kernel sizes in Diffuser improve performance but harm generalization. This analysis provides useful insights into why the hierarchical decomposition helps. Additionally, the SD variant with dense actions (SD-DA) effectively addresses a known bottleneck in hierarchical RL by improving the fitting of sparse objectives through intermediate action supervision.

The computational speedup achieved by HD is notable—shorter high-level sequences combined with parallelizable low-level planning provide practical benefits. The experimental evaluation is comprehensive, spanning Maze2D, AntMaze, MuJoCo, and FrankaKitchen environments with appropriate ablation studies.

## Major Weaknesses

The primary concern is **limited novelty**. The approach essentially combines two standard diffusion models without introducing substantial algorithmic innovation. This straightforward stacking of diffusers, while empirically effective, does not constitute a significant conceptual advance over existing hierarchical RL and diffusion-based planning literature.

More critically, the paper lacks a principled treatment of **sub-goal independence**. Sub-goals are generated without explicit knowledge of the final objective or consideration of how segments interact. This myopic planning could lead to non-optimal or even infeasible solutions. There is no mechanism to prevent path overlap between segments or to ensure that the concatenation of independently solved segments produces coherent long-horizon behavior. The paper provides no analysis of how robust the approach is when low-level planners fail to reach intended sub-goals due to action-to-state mapping errors.

**Technical clarity is insufficient** on several fronts. The paper does not clearly specify whether diffusion-sampled actions or a separate controller are used for Maze2D tasks. The number of trajectories sampled for individual segments and sub-goal sequences remains unspecified. Critically, there is no feedback mechanism from the low-level diffuser back to the high-level planner—this one-directional information flow seems like a significant limitation that is not adequately discussed.

## Minor Weaknesses

The paper would benefit from additional ablations. Specifically, comparisons with state-only diffusion approaches (e.g., Decision-Diffuser) are missing, making it unclear whether both state and action information are truly necessary for the hierarchical formulation. The claim that increased receptive fields are uniquely beneficial to the hierarchical approach is undermined by the finding that larger Diffuser kernels also improve performance, suggesting the gains may be partially attributable to this simpler mechanism rather than the hierarchical structure itself.

## Missing Details and Suggestions

To strengthen the work, the authors should:

1. **Justify design choices** through ablations comparing state-only diffusion variants to clarify why both states and actions are essential.

2. **Clarify implementation details**, particularly: (a) whether Maze2D tasks use diffusion-sampled actions or a separate controller, and (b) the exact number of trajectories sampled during segment and sub-goal sequence generation.

3. **Address robustness concerns** by discussing and analyzing how the method handles cases where low-level actions fail to reach intended sub-goals, and explain any mechanisms (if present) to prevent or handle path overlap between segments.

4. **Consider feedback mechanisms** that allow the low-level diffuser to inform or correct high-level planning, potentially improving the coherence of hierarchical decomposition.

5. **Provide theoretical or empirical analysis** demonstrating that the benefits of the hierarchical approach genuinely stem from the hierarchical structure rather than simply from increased effective receptive field or other confounding factors.

## Overall Assessment

Hierarchical Diffuser presents a pragmatic approach to extending diffusion-based planning with meaningful empirical improvements. The experimental validation is reasonably comprehensive, and the computational benefits are tangible. However, the work is fundamentally limited by its incremental nature—it amounts to a straightforward combination of existing techniques without significant algorithmic or conceptual innovation. The independent planning of sub-goals without feedback or goal-awareness represents a conceptual weakness that is inadequately addressed. While the empirical results push the work slightly above the acceptance threshold, the limited novelty and unresolved technical concerns prevent this from being a strong contribution.

The paper makes a solid empirical contribution that will be of interest to practitioners working with diffusion-based RL, but it falls short of the innovation expected for a strong acceptance. With revisions addressing the feedback mechanisms, sub-goal independence concerns, and technical clarity, this work could be substantially strengthened.

## Recommendation

**Accept (marginally above threshold)**

The empirical improvements and comprehensive experiments justify publication, but the limited novelty and conceptual concerns should be noted. The work advances the practical state of diffusion-based planning but does not constitute a significant methodological innovation.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes an hierarchical variant of Diffuser. The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. The authers also study the impact of kernel size in Diffuser and the impact of the jumpy steps. An interesting observation is that Diffuser with a small receptive field can not cover the data distribution well.
reviews.strengths:
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.

reviews.weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Hierarchical Diffuser

## Summary

This paper introduces Hierarchical Diffuser, a hierarchical variant of the Diffuser algorithm for offline reinforcement learning and long-horizon planning. The approach decomposes planning into two levels: a high-level sparse planner that operates on subsampled trajectories and a low-level detailed planner that refines trajectories between subgoals. The work demonstrates competitive empirical results on long-horizon tasks and provides interesting analysis regarding the relationship between the diffusion model's receptive field and data distribution coverage.

## Strengths

1. **Clear presentation**: The paper is well-written with a logical flow that makes the proposed method easy to understand and follow.

2. **Strong empirical performance**: The method shows substantial improvements over the baseline Diffuser algorithm, particularly on challenging long-horizon planning tasks like AntMaze. The complete failure of standard Diffuser on AntMaze-Large versus the success of Hierarchical Diffuser is a compelling demonstration of the approach's effectiveness.

3. **Valuable insights on receptive fields**: The investigation into how receptive field size relates to data distribution coverage is a noteworthy contribution. The empirical observation that Diffuser with small receptive fields fails to adequately cover the data distribution—despite having sufficient model capacity—provides useful guidance for future research on diffusion-based planning methods.

4. **Practical design**: The use of simple fixed-interval subgoal selection rather than complex adaptive procedures makes the method straightforward to implement and reproduce.

## Weaknesses

1. **Incomplete baseline comparisons**: The paper does not include Decision Diffuser as a baseline for comparison. This is a significant omission, as Decision Diffuser represents an important related approach that should be evaluated against the proposed method.

2. **Limited architectural exploration**: The work focuses exclusively on CNN architectures as used in the original Diffuser. A more comprehensive study comparing CNN and transformer-based architectures (such as those employed in Decision Diffuser) would strengthen the contribution and provide broader insights into which architectural choices are most suitable for hierarchical planning.

## Technical Comments

The hierarchical decomposition is straightforward: the high-level planner operates on subsampled trajectories for computational efficiency, while the low-level planner handles refinement between adjacent subgoals. The parallel training of both planners and the inclusion of variants with dense actions (HD-DA and SD-DA) for improved return prediction are sensible design choices.

However, some questions remain unanswered. The paper raises but does not fully resolve whether standard Diffuser's failures stem from inadequate distribution coverage during training or from execution-time issues. Similarly, while Hierarchical Diffuser generally achieves better coverage, the observation that it still fails to cover certain short segments or branches in the data distribution deserves deeper investigation.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Add Decision Diffuser baseline**: Include Decision Diffuser in the experimental comparisons to provide a more complete evaluation of the method's relative performance.

2. **Explore architectural variants**: Conduct experiments comparing CNN and transformer architectures within the hierarchical framework to determine whether the improvements are architecture-agnostic or specific to CNNs.

3. **Deepen analysis of coverage gaps**: Provide more detailed investigation into why certain segments of the data distribution remain uncovered and whether this impacts downstream task performance.

## Minor Comments

The technical details are generally sound, and the empirical methodology appears appropriate. The variants with dense actions represent a useful extension, though their contribution relative to the core hierarchical approach could be better isolated.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a solid contribution to offline RL and planning with diffusion models. The hierarchical decomposition is intuitive and effective, and the empirical results on long-horizon tasks are convincing. The analysis of receptive field effects on distribution coverage provides valuable insights for the community. However, the work is held back by incomplete comparisons with related methods (particularly Decision Diffuser) and limited exploration of architectural alternatives. These gaps prevent this from being a strong accept, though the core contributions and strong empirical results on challenging domains warrant acceptance.

**Recommendation: Accept (marginally above acceptance threshold)**

The paper presents useful ideas and demonstrates practical improvements, but addressing the missing baselines and expanding the architectural analysis would significantly strengthen the contribution.

reviews.summary: The authors of this paper present Hierarchical Diffuser, a simple yet effective hierarchical planning approach using diffusion models to improve the performance and efficiency of diffusion-based planning for long-horizon tasks. By utilizing a two-pronged structure, the Hierarchical Diffuser combines a high-level planner, the Sparse Diffuser, to generate sub-goals, and a low-level planner to refine the plan. This approach significantly outperforms non-hierarchical planning methods, while also enhancing computational efficiency in training and planning speed. Furthermore, the method displays improved generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks compared to non-hierarchical Diffusers.
reviews.strengths:
- Originality: This work creatively combines the strengths of hierarchical planning and diffusion models, achieving significant improvements in performance and computational efficiency compared to existing methods.
- Quality: The contribution provides a thorough theoretical analysis of the method, backed by well-designed experiments and in-depth analyses. The authors emphasize the rationale behind the design decisions and the resultant benefits of their approach.
- Clarity: The paper is well-organized, clearly written, and explains the method along with its advantages and limitations in a coherent and accessible way.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Considering the existence of the HDMI algorithm, the contribution of this paper are limited. However, compared to HDMI, this paper has conducted extensive experiments on generalization and provided corresponding theoretical support. One suggestion I have is that the authors can emphasize the advantages of Hierarchical Diffuser from the perspective of generalization, and highlight the algorithm's generalization performance in the experimental design.
- In the hierarchical framework, the quality of the generated high-level goals is crucial to the effectiveness of the algorithm. The authors emphasize that the Hierarchical Diffuser is simpler compared to existing hierarchical methods but have only designed one goal selection method. In addition to the fixed time interval heuristic method used in this paper, HDMI also mentions two heuristic methods based on spatial intervals and reward scale, which are equally simple and have low implementation costs. If the authors could take these heuristic methods into consideration, I believe it would have a positive impact on the completeness of the paper's content. Furthermore, the demonstration of the quality of the generated goals is essential, and there is a gap in this aspect in the current paper.
reviews.questions:
- I have noticed that both Diffuser and Decision Diffuser employ “inpainting” techniques for trajectory generation in goal-conditioned tasks and the lower-level planner of HDMI. However, this paper utilizes return-guided sampling. What were the considerations behind the authors' choice between the two methods?
- This paper proposes to get the sub-goals first with the planning-based subgoal extractor and then train HDMI with the sub-goals as supervision. Although this two-step process makes sense, the overall training process becomes ad hoc and less general. Is there any connection between the upper and lower layers of Hierarchical Diffuser's planner? Can it be trained end-to-end?

Summary:
The authors of this paper present Hierarchical Diffuser, a simple yet effective hierarchical planning approach using diffusion models to improve the performance and efficiency of diffusion-based planning for long-horizon tasks. By utilizing a two-pronged structure, the Hierarchical Diffuser combines a high-level planner, the Sparse Diffuser, to generate sub-goals, and a low-level planner to refine the plan. This approach significantly outperforms non-hierarchical planning methods, while also enhancing computational efficiency in training and planning speed. Furthermore, the method displays improved generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks compared to non-hierarchical Diffusers.

Strengths:
- Originality: This work creatively combines the strengths of hierarchical planning and diffusion models, achieving significant improvements in performance and computational efficiency compared to existing methods.
- Quality: The contribution provides a thorough theoretical analysis of the method, backed by well-designed experiments and in-depth analyses. The authors emphasize the rationale behind the design decisions and the resultant benefits of their approach.
- Clarity: The paper is well-organized, clearly written, and explains the method along with its advantages and limitations in a coherent and accessible way.

Weaknesses:
- Considering the existence of the HDMI algorithm, the contribution of this paper are limited. However, compared to HDMI, this paper has conducted extensive experiments on generalization and provided corresponding theoretical support. One suggestion I have is that the authors can emphasize the advantages of Hierarchical Diffuser from the perspective of generalization, and highlight the algorithm's generalization performance in the experimental design.
- In the hierarchical framework, the quality of the generated high-level goals is crucial to the effectiveness of the algorithm. The authors emphasize that the Hierarchical Diffuser is simpler compared to existing hierarchical methods but have only designed one goal selection method. In addition to the fixed time interval heuristic method used in this paper, HDMI also mentions two heuristic methods based on spatial intervals and reward scale, which are equally simple and have low implementation costs. If the authors could take these heuristic methods into consideration, I believe it would have a positive impact on the completeness of the paper's content. Furthermore, the demonstration of the quality of the generated goals is essential, and there is a gap in this aspect in the current paper.

Questions:
- I have noticed that both Diffuser and Decision Diffuser employ “inpainting” techniques for trajectory generation in goal-conditioned tasks and the lower-level planner of HDMI. However, this paper utilizes return-guided sampling. What were the considerations behind the authors' choice between the two methods?

Although this two-step process makes sense, the overall training process becomes ad hoc and less general. Is there any connection between the upper and lower layers of Hierarchical Diffuser's planner? Can it be trained end-to-end?
